subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $A$ be the event that player A is selected as captain, and $B$ the event that player B is selected as captain. Let $W$ be the event that the team wins the tournament.

Given:
$P(A) = 0.6$, $P(B) = 0.4$
$P(W \mid A) = 0.8$, $P(W \mid B) = 0.7$

By the law of total probability:
$P(W) = P(A) \cdot P(W \mid A) + P(B) \cdot P(W \mid B)$

$P(W) = (0.6)(0.8) + (0.4)(0.7)$

$P(W) = 0.48 + 0.28 = 0.76$

Hence, the correct option is $(2)\ 0.76$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $h$ be the number of heads and $t$ be the number of tails obtained.

The total points obtained is $10h + 5t$.

For the man to get exactly $30$ points, we must have:

$10h + 5t = 30 \Rightarrow 2h + t = 6$

Since $h$ and $t$ are non-negative integers, the possible pairs of $(h, t)$ are $(3, 0)$, $(2, 2)$, $(1, 4)$, and $(0, 6)$.

For each pair, the number of possible sequences of heads and tails is given by $^{h+t}C_{h}$, and the probability of any specific sequence is $\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{h+t}$.

The total probability is the sum of the probabilities of all these mutually exclusive cases:

For $h = 3, t = 0$, the probability is $^{3}C_{3} \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{3} = 1 \times \dfrac{1}{8} = \dfrac{8}{64}$.

For $h = 2, t = 2$, the probability is $^{4}C_{2} \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{4} = 6 \times \dfrac{1}{16} = \dfrac{24}{64}$.

For $h = 1, t = 4$, the probability is $^{5}C_{1} \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{5} = 5 \times \dfrac{1}{32} = \dfrac{10}{64}$.

For $h = 0, t = 6$, the probability is $^{6}C_{0} \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{6} = 1 \times \dfrac{1}{64} = \dfrac{1}{64}$.

Total probability $= \dfrac{8}{64} + \dfrac{24}{64} + \dfrac{10}{64} + \dfrac{1}{64} = \dfrac{43}{64}$.

Thus, $m = 43$ and $n = 64$.

Since $\gcd(43, 64) = 1$, the fraction is in its simplest form.

$m + n = 43 + 64 = 107$.

Answer: $107$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The probability of getting a 4 in a throw of a die is 16.

The probability of getting other than 4 i.e. 4 i.e. numbers 1, 2, 3, 5& 6 in a single throw of a die is 56.

Total throws done =5.

For the event to be completed in 5th throw, 4th and 5th throw must be 4 . Also, 3rd throw must be other than 4 i.e. 4. Hence, cases for the first two position can be :

Case I : 1st throw must be 4 and 2nd throw must not be 4 i.e. 4

Thus, P44-4-44=16.56.56.16.16=2565.

Case II : 1st throw must not be 4 i.e.  4 and 2nd throw must not be 4 i.e. 4

Thus, P4-4-4-44=56.56.56.16.16=12565.

Case III : 1st throw must not be 4 i.e. 4 and 2nd throw must be 4

Thus, P4-44-44=56.16.56.16.16=2565.

Therefore, probability that experiment will end in the fifth throw given that two fours occurred in succession will be

P44-4-44+P4-44-44+P4-4-4-44

=2565+2565+12565

=17565.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $E_1$ be the event that the letter came from KANPUR and $E_2$ be the event that the letter came from ANANTPUR.

Since the letter is equally likely to come from either city, $P(E_1) = P(E_2) = \dfrac{1}{2}$.

Let $A$ be the event that the two consecutive visible letters are AN.

In the word KANPUR, there are 6 letters, so there are 5 pairs of consecutive letters: KA, AN, NP, PU, UR. The pair AN appears exactly once. Thus, $P(A|E_1) = \dfrac{1}{5}$.

In the word ANANTPUR, there are 8 letters, so there are 7 pairs of consecutive letters: AN, NA, AN, NT, TP, PU, UR. The pair AN appears exactly twice. Thus, $P(A|E_2) = \dfrac{2}{7}$.

By Bayes' theorem, the probability that the letter came from ANANTPUR given that AN is visible is:

$P(E_2|A) = \dfrac{P(E_2) P(A|E_2)}{P(E_1) P(A|E_1) + P(E_2) P(A|E_2)}$

$P(E_2|A) = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{2}{7}}{\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{5} + \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{2}{7}}$

$P(E_2|A) = \dfrac{\dfrac{2}{7}}{\dfrac{1}{5} + \dfrac{2}{7}}$

$P(E_2|A) = \dfrac{\dfrac{2}{7}}{\dfrac{17}{35}} = \dfrac{2}{7} \times \dfrac{35}{17} = \dfrac{10}{17}$

Answer: $\dfrac{10}{17}$

Taking D15 as 15 ! e, D(14) as 14!e and D(13) as 13!e

We get , 1-15!e+15·14!e+15×142×13!e15!

=1-1e+1e+12e=1-52e≈.08

solution:
Given,

A fair n n>1 faces die is rolled repeatedly until a number less than n appears,

xi      1                2                         3                      4                       ...pi   n-1n     1n·n-1n      1n2·n-1n      1n3·n-1n     ...

Now we know that, mean is given by,

Mean =∑i=1∞pixi=1·n-1n+2n·n-1n+3n2 n-1n+...

⇒n9=1-1nS ........1

Where, S=1+2n+3n2+4n3+...  ......2

1nS=1n+2n2+3n3+...  ......3

Now subtracting equation 2-3 we get,

1-1nS=1+1n+1n2+1n3+...

⇒1-1nS=11-1n

Now putting the value of S in equation 1 we get,

⇒n9=1-1n×11-1n2=nn-1

⇒n=10

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given,

In a city, 25% of the population is smoker

So, Psmoker=0.25

Pnon-smoking=0.75

And smoker has 27 times more chance of being diagnosed with lung cancer,

So, if PLung cancernon-smoker=p then PLung cancersmoker=27p,

Now,  a person is selected at random and found to be diagnosed with lung cancer,

So, total probability of having a lung cancer will be 

PLung cancer=0.25×27p+0.75×p

So the probability of him being smoker (using bayes' theorem) will be PsmokerLung cancer=0.25×27p0.25×27p+0.75×p=2730=910

Now comparing with k10=910,

Then the value of k will be 9.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Total number of five-digit numbers are 9104.

Case I : Choosing two non-zero digits i.e. in C29 ways.

So, number of five-digit numbers containing both digits =25-2.

Case II : Choose one non-zero and one zero as digit i.e. in C19 ways.

So, number of five-digit numbers containing one non-zero and one zero both =24-1.

So, favourable number of ways =C2925-2+C1924-1=3630+915=1080+135=1215

Thus, required probability =12159×104=135104.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given:

P(A¯∩B)+P(A∩B¯)=1-k

P(A¯∩C)+P(A∩C¯)=1-k

P(B¯∩C)+P(B∩C¯)=1-2k

P(A∩B∩C)=k2

Now,

P(A)+P(B)-2P(A∩B)=1-k …i

P(B)+P(C)-2P(B∩C)=1-2k …ii

P(C)+P(A)-2P(A∩C)=1-k …iii

Adding i, ii & iii, we get

PA+PB+PC-PA∩B-PB∩C-PC∩A=-4k+32

Now,

PA∪B∪C=PA+PB+PC-PA∩B-PB∩C-PC∩A+PA∩B∩C

⇒PA∪B∪C=-4k+32+k2

⇒PA∪B∪C=2k2-4k+32

⇒PA∪B∪C=2(k-1)2+12

⇒PA∪B∪C=k-12+12

⇒P(A∪B∪C)>12

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Since $\mathrm{X}$ has a binomial distribution, $\mathrm{B}(n$,
p)
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\therefore \mathrm{P}(X=2)={ }^n \mathrm{C}_2(p)^2(1-p)^{n-2} \\
&\text { and } \mathrm{P}(X=3)={ }^n \mathrm{C}_3(p)^3(1-p)^{n-3} \\
&\text { Given } \mathrm{P}(X=2)=\mathrm{P}(X=3) \\
&\Rightarrow{ }^n \mathrm{C}_2 p^2(1-p)^{n-2}={ }^n \mathrm{C}_3(p)^3(1-p)^{n-3} \\
&\Rightarrow \frac{n !}{2 !(n-2) !} \cdot \frac{p^2(1-p)^n}{(1-p)^2} \\
&=\frac{n !}{3 !(n-3) !} \cdot \frac{p^3(1-p)^n}{(1-p)^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\Rightarrow \frac{1}{n-2}=\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{p}{1-p} \\
&\Rightarrow 3(1-p)=p(n-2) \\
&\Rightarrow 3-3 p=n p-2 p \\
&\Rightarrow n p=3-p \\
&\Rightarrow \mathrm{E}(X)=\text { mean }=3-p \\
&(\because \text { mean of } \mathrm{B}(n, p)=n p)
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Total No of 3-digit Number =900

Case -1 When there are 2 odd digit and 1 even digit

(i) Even odd odd =4×5×5=100

{As 0 cannot come at 1st place}

(ii) Odd even odd =5×5×5=125

(iii) Odd odd even =5×5×5=125

Case-2

All 3 odd digit =5×5×5=125

So total number of selecting 3-digit number having atleast 2 odd digit will be addition of both cases which is=100+125+125+125=475

So probability =475900=1936
